title: Go Stack vs Heap
content:
### Stack vs Heap in Go

Understanding memory allocation helps write efficient Go code.

### Stack

- Fast, automatic memory management
- Function call frames are pushed/popped
- Limited size (goroutines start with 2KB, grows as needed)
- No garbage collection overhead

### Heap

- Slower, managed by garbage collector
- For data that outlives the function scope
- Requires GC cycles to clean up

### Escape Analysis

Go's compiler decides where to allocate memory:

```go
func onStack() int {
    x := 42     // stays on stack
    return x
}

func onHeap() *int {
    x := 42     // escapes to heap!
    return &x
}
```

### When Does Escape Happen?

| Situation | Allocation |
|-----------|------------|
| Returning pointer to local | Heap |
| Storing pointer in interface | Heap |
| Variable too large for stack | Heap |
| Variable used in closure | Heap |
| Pointer passed to function that stores it | Heap |

### Checking Escape Analysis

```bash
go build -gcflags='-m' main.go
```

### Optimization Tips

```go
// Inefficient - returns slice reference
func readFile() []byte {
    data := make([]byte, 1000)
    // ...
    return data // escapes to heap
}

// Better - pass buffer in
func readFile(buf []byte) []byte {
    // use provided buffer
    return buf
}
```

### Pointer vs Value Trade-off

```go
// Pointer - may cause escape
func newPerson(name string) *Person {
    return &Person{Name: name} // escapes
}

// Value - stays on stack (if small)
func makePerson(name string) Person {
    return Person{Name: name} // on stack
}
```

> **Key Point:** Go's escape analysis makes allocation decisions automatically. Focus on writing clear code first, optimize allocations only when profiling shows issues.

quiz.question: What is escape analysis?
options: Compile-time decision of stack vs heap
